author: @lisakingscott7729
text:
30 volts at 100mA is 3W. The diameter looks like it's about 0.5m, so say 0.2m2 area and 3.3 m/s wind speed.
The power in the wind is 0.5 x mass x velocity^2 = 0.5 x swept area x air density x velocity^3
So at 3.4m/s the power in watts is 0.5 x 0.2 x 1.23 x 3.4^3 = 4.8W
So the efficiency does look pretty good, something like 62% assuming the wind speed was correct. Ideally the anemometer should have been raised on a parallel broomstick, as power is proportional to the cube of the windspeed.
But that's pretty good. I'm playing with designs for wind turbines on a pitched roof ridge, to benefit from the compression effect of the wind over the pitched roof, using variants of shrouded Savonius and Darius turbines on their side.

author: @sailingoctopus1
text: I heard about a guy that was building a HAWT that instead of having vertical aerofoils had Flettner rotors, the idea being that whereas half the aerofoils on a HAWT are causing drag as they move against the direction of the wind with a series of Flettner rotors they would all be providing drive all of the time. I seem to remember that a complicated set of cogs were used to ensure that each Flettner rotor was providing drive in the required direction, I'm not sure how. I believe the vertical Flettner rotors were actually fixed cylindrical structures between the two horizontal discs, but that it was only the surface of the cylinder that rotated, not the whole cylinder. I think this was intended to increase structural rigidity of the HAWT. Interestingly, to reduce the load on the central bearings, the outer circumference of the lower disc ran on a circular rail. I wish I could remember more, but it intrigued me at the time. Remembering this the other day, I thought I bet that is something Rob would like to try.

author: @trevorgoddard2278
text:
I think saying exactly 60 degrees is an unwarranted assumption as the gain in efficiency comes from the rear set avoiding the turbulent air from the front set.
To me that would imply that the angular offset should depend on the windspeed and/or the rotational speed of the blades.

replies.author: @trevorgoddard2278
replies.text:
As usual just typing my observations led me to two additional thoughts.
1. Using a differential gearbox with the generators rotor attached to the body and each blade set attached to what would be the axles would allow the offset to set itself.
2. Using counter-rotating blade sets (with appropriate gears to achieve that) might produce a compromise efficiency gain.

author: @AndrewWalsh-x2s
text:
Morning, 
Apologies if I am teaching to suck eggs, but if a dual rotor system is used for a wind turbine then a stator section will massively increase the efficiency of the second . This is one of the purposes within  the compressor stage within an axial flow compressor (as used in a jet engine). 
Hope this is of interest.
Kindest regards
Andy Walsh
P.S keep up the good work

author: @kadmow
text: yep, Betz relies on the calculation at an unconstrained disk - as soon as many disks, or ducts enter, the calculations per generator are invalid - ie. Betz becomes irrelevant, new metrics and limits enter the discussion (the energy obtained from a flowing fluid is still limited - in that, if too much energy is extracted, less flow occurs limiting available energy - intuition may be more useful than specific values - regardless of Betz or not, the most energy with the lowest input of materials and energy - lowest cost - is worth chasing.  More blades are only a commercial concern/interest once all available rotor diameters are exhausted..)

author: @Johnson_Rice
text:
Hear me out... what if it was NOT horizontal, but still vertical, HOWEVER below a Darwin Wind Collector... that was feeding its airflow into a rose turbine/vortex generator (which is why I am thinking it would still need to be vertical, but at ground level, catching the vortex being exhausted....  (Something like from 1352)  I've been mulling this for a bit but my living situation and lack of 3d printer is making me sad. I know Rose Turbines are typically for liquids, but I've been wondering if you combined a Darwin Wind Collector and a Rose Vortex Generator duct system.... would that generate more or less than JUST the Waters Turbine Betz Beater from 1921?  I'm even wondering if getting the air spinning into a vortex by the time it hits the waters turbine would make that turbine spin even faster... 

I have no way personally of testing my ideas right now but if anyone does... I'd love to hear about it.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: i said that in the vid mate

replies.author: @Johnson_Rice
replies.text:
You did? I heard mention of the darwin wind turbine... but no mention of the rose vortex being combined in there... That's the main crux of my pondering here... Could a rose vortex (like from 1352) be combined with a darwin wind collector becuase of the way it directs the wind...?   If a Darwin wind turbine was feeding into a rose vortex turbine (with this dual rotor turbine catching the output) - I was thinking the rose turbine shape would necessitate the fan blades being vertical again on the side catching the exhaust....  (Sort of like how the tesla turbine makes things seem a little sideways) 
  I'll watch again and listen harder@ThinkingandTinkering

author: @cahito00
text: Great achievement! I know you’re probably doing the Darwin arrangement first, but could (or would) you investigate on putting that blade arrangement in a propeller? I know people made counter rotating propellers to improve efficiency, but what if you build them like this generator? Would it improve the thrust? I think it would deserve a look on it… don’t you think?!?

replies.author: @TheSorters
replies.text: I thought the same thing. Does more watts per a given wind speed equal more thrust for a given input power. Is this a symmetrical problem?

replies.author: @TheSorters
replies.text: @paradiselost9946 yes, I am an aerospace engineer doing CFD, I understand a wind turbine slows air down, and a propellor speeds it up, but why wouldn't they be symmetric depending on if power is extracted or added. An inefficient turbine is probably an inefficient propellor. You are wrong on several points. For instance, yes, counter rotating blades do increase efficiency, ask the Russians. Yes, ducts do increase efficiency until they add more drag, and yes, number of blades matter. Number of turbine blades in a jet engine matter too

replies.author: @YoureNowOnTV
replies.text: One reason is volume of sales. If everyone wants one, manufacturers will compete on price and we get a consumer end benefit from that competition in price.

He had both 3 blade designs and old prairie water pumping windmil designs he could produce fair amounts of electricity down to 3-5mph

author: @chiritaalex
text: you need two sets of blades spinning in opposite directions, and one is connected to the rotor and the other to the stator, to double the velocity.

replies.author: @merseyviking
replies.text: I'm not sure that's going to be very efficient because the leeward blades are going to be crossing the dirty air off the back of the windward blades. That said, there are drones that use coaxial counter-rotating blades, so it's not a complete write-off, but a quick Google shows that the thrust generated by the lower rotor is about half that of the upper rotor. I guess it goes back to what Robert was saying in a previous video about whether you have a torquey motor, or a speedy motor. Counter-rotating blades would (I guess) be better for a motor designed for speed. Definitely worth an experiment though.

replies.author: @EGDD-42
replies.text: A contra rotating system? You get more thrust in contra-rotating aircraft, but the gear arrangement is an engineer's nightmare
